"Robert le Diable," "Les Huguenots," or "Guillaume Tell" usually took up three evenings, so slow was the execution of these masterpieces. The _vivaces_, at the theatre of Quiquendone, lagged like real _adagios_. The _allegros_ were "long-drawn out" indeed. The demisemiquavers were scarcely equal to the ordinary semibreves of other countries. The most rapid runs, performed according to Quiquendonian taste, had the solemn march of a chant. The gayest shakes were languishing and measured, that they might not shock the ears of the _dilettanti_. The leading malcontent was Hearne, a sealing-master, to whom his companions were ready to listen. He had found it easy to get the upper hand of the other sailors by his rough manner and the surly audacity that was expressed by his whole personality. Hearne was an American, and forty-five years of age. He was an active, vigorous man, and I could see him in my mind's eye, standing up on his double bowed whaling-boat brandishing the harpoon, darting it into the flank of a whale, and paying out the rope. He must have been fine to see. Granted his passion for this business, I could not be surprised that his discontent showed itself upon occasion. [AWED-010-003]But British India, properly so called, only embraces seven hundred thousand square miles, and a population of from one hundred to one hundred and ten millions of inhabitants. A considerable portion of India is still free from British authority; and there are certain ferocious rajahs in the interior who are absolutely independent. The celebrated East India Company was all-powerful from 1756, when the English first gained a foothold on the spot where now stands the city of Madras, down to the time of the great Sepoy insurrection. It gradually annexed province after province, purchasing them of the native chiefs, whom it seldom paid, and appointed the governor-general and his subordinates, civil and military. But the East India Company has now passed away, leaving the British possessions in India directly under the control of the Crown. The aspect of the country, as well as the manners and distinctions of race, is daily changing.

The moon--the planets would say to him, "There, on that point of the ocean, is thy ship!" That firmament, on which the stars move like the hands of a perfect clock, which nothing shakes nor can derange, and whose accuracy is absolute--that firmament would tell him the hours and the distances. Hatteras's character is fused with his nationality and his fixed idea. He is proud of being an Englishman (ACH-012-047), and declares of any capable man that he should like to be an Englishman (ACH-012-048), measuring worth by that standard. This pride, welded to his singular aim — to attain the Pole was the aim of his life (ACH-012-056) — makes him at once heroic and dangerously obsessive, driving his men toward a goal that consumes him utterly.
- Manservant to [[Phileas Fogg]], whom he serves throughout the wager.

Top is the dog of Cyrus Harding and one of the loyal companions of the Lincoln Island castaways. Introduced as a favorite of the engineer who leaps into the balloon car with the party (MI-002-046), Top is a constant presence through the colony's ordeals, and his animal instincts more than once alert the men to unseen dangers.

Verne anticipates weightlessness at the gravitational neutral point, where the travellers float: passing it, [[barbicane|Barbicane]] explains, "the lunar attraction will draw us to the moon" (RM-008-042). The projectile's failure to reach its target is treated as an orbital-mechanics problem, its deviation caused when another body's "attraction was sufficient to have an influence upon our direction" (RM-009-050).

In *Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea* the Abraham Lincoln takes the long way south in her hunt for the monster: "Commander Farragut was unwilling to attempt this tortuous passageway and maneuvered instead to double Cape Horn." (TTLU-005-005) On July 6 the frigate "doubled that solitary islet at the tip of the South American continent." (TTLU-005-007)
id: easter-island
For all its raw energy it wears a familiar face to the travellers, so that at the International Hotel Passepartout felt as if he "had not... left England at all." (AWED-025-007)

Tijuco is a real town in Brazil's diamond district, the scene of the old crime that hangs over Joam Dacosta throughout Eight Hundred Leagues on the Amazon. Once the most forward in accusing him as the principal author of the crime of Tijuco, some now aver that he is not guilty. (EHLA-034-001)

Its coast runs the length of the continent — that long coast line which extends from Peru to Chili. (DSCF-013-047) It is a land of catastrophe: one of those cataclysms frequent in Chili, in the very region where Copiapo had been twice destroyed and Santiago four times laid in ruins in fourteen years. (SC-013-073)
